title: Noun Gender — el and la
summary: Every Spanish noun is either masculine or feminine. Gender controls the articles and adjectives around the noun, so it must be learned with each word.
intro: Unlike English, every single Spanish noun carries a grammatical gender: it is either masculine or feminine. This has little to do with biology — a table (la mesa) is feminine and a book (el libro) is masculine. Gender is simply a property of the word itself.

Why does it matter? Because the words that travel with a noun — articles (el, la, un, una) and adjectives (rojo/roja, bueno/buena) — must agree with that gender. If you get the gender wrong, everything attached to the noun sounds wrong too.

The practical advice every good learner follows: never memorise a noun alone. Learn it together with its article. Don't learn 'mesa' — learn 'la mesa'. Don't learn 'libro' — learn 'el libro'. The article becomes part of the word in your memory.
formula: el + masculine noun  •  la + feminine noun

sections.heading: Endings that usually signal masculine
body: These endings are reliable clues, though not absolute rules. They will be right the large majority of the time.
bullets: -o → el libro (book), el perro (dog), el dinero (money), el coche (car)
-or → el profesor (teacher), el amor (love), el color (colour)
-aje → el viaje (trip), el paisaje (landscape), el mensaje (message)
-ma/-pa/-ta from Greek → el problema, el tema, el idioma, el mapa, el planeta

sections.heading: Endings that usually signal feminine
body: Just like the masculine clues, these point you the right way most of the time.
bullets: -a → la casa (house), la mesa (table), la palabra (word), la silla (chair)
-ción / -sión → la nación (nation), la canción (song), la decisión (decision)
-dad / -tad → la ciudad (city), la verdad (truth), la libertad (freedom)
-tud / -umbre → la actitud (attitude), la costumbre (custom)

examples.es: El libro nuevo está en la mesa.
examples.en: The new book is on the table.

examples.note: problema is masculine despite ending in -a

sections.heading: Nouns for people: the same word, two genders
body: Many nouns referring to people change ending to match the person, and the article changes too.
bullets: el profesor / la profesora (the (male/female) teacher)
el niño / la niña (the boy / the girl)
el gato / la gata (the (male/female) cat)
Some don't change form, only the article: el estudiante / la estudiante, el artista / la artista

examples.es: Mi hermano es médico y mi hermana es médica.
examples.en: My brother is a doctor and my sister is a doctor.
examples.note: the profession agrees with the person's gender
estudiante doesn't change; only the article (la) shows gender

sections.heading: Tricky exceptions worth memorising
body: A handful of common words break the ending rules. Because they are frequent, it pays to learn them early.
bullets: el día (day), el mapa (map), el sofá (sofa) — end in -a but masculine
la mano (hand), la foto (photo), la moto (motorbike) — end in -o but feminine
Feminine nouns starting with a stressed a- use el/un for sound: el agua, el águila, un hacha — but they stay feminine (el agua está fría)

examples.es: El día es largo y la mano está fría.
examples.en: The day is long and the hand is cold.
examples.note: el día (masc.), la mano (fem.) — both break the rule
el agua for sound, but feminine → fría, not frío

mistakes.note: Greek-origin -ma words are masculine.
mano is feminine even though it ends in -o.
agua takes el for sound but stays feminine, so the adjective is fría.
día is masculine despite the -a ending.

title: Articles — el, la, los, las / un, una
summary: Definite (the) and indefinite (a/an/some) articles change for both gender and number, and two of them contract with a and de.
intro: In English there is one definite article (the) and the indefinite a/an. Spanish has a fuller set: articles change to match the gender (masculine/feminine) and number (singular/plural) of the noun they introduce.
This is actually helpful — the article gives you information about the noun before you even reach it. If you hear 'las', you already know what follows is feminine and plural.
There are also two small but very frequent contractions (al and del) and a special rule for feminine words that begin with a stressed a- sound.
formula: Definite: el / la / los / las  •  Indefinite: un / una / unos / unas

sections.heading: Definite articles — 'the'
body: Use these for something specific or already known, and also for nouns used in a general sense (which English often leaves bare).
bullets: el — masculine singular: el coche (the car)
la — feminine singular: la silla (the chair)
los — masculine plural: los coches (the cars)
las — feminine plural: las sillas (the chairs)

examples.es: El coche de mi padre es azul.

examples.note: general statement: Spanish keeps the article (los) where English drops it

sections.heading: Indefinite articles — 'a / an / some'
body: Use these for something new or non-specific. In the plural they mean 'some' or 'a few'.
bullets: un — masculine singular: un libro (a book)
una — feminine singular: una casa (a house)
unos — masculine plural: unos libros (some books)
unas — feminine plural: unas casas (some houses)

examples.es: Hay unas personas en la puerta.
examples.en: There are some people at the door.

sections.heading: The two contractions: al and del
body: When the masculine 'el' follows the prepositions a (to) or de (of/from), the two words merge into one. This is obligatory, not optional. Note it only happens with el — never with la, los, las.
bullets: a + el = al → Voy al cine. (I'm going to the cinema.)
de + el = del → La puerta del coche. (The door of the car.)
No contraction with the others: a la, a los, a las, de la, de los, de las

examples.es: Vamos al parque y volvemos del trabajo tarde.
examples.en: We go to the park and come back from work late.

sections.heading: When Spanish omits the article
body: Sometimes Spanish drops the article where you might expect one — especially with professions and quantities.
bullets: Unmodified professions after ser: Soy profesor. (I am a teacher.) — no un
But add the article if you describe it: Soy un buen profesor.
After hay (there is/are) with plurals: Hay libros en la mesa.

sections.heading: Feminine words starting with stressed a-
body: To avoid two a-sounds clashing, feminine singular nouns that begin with a stressed a- or ha- take el/un instead of la/una. The noun stays feminine, and the plural goes back to las/unas.
bullets: el agua (but: las aguas), el águila (the eagle), el hacha (the axe), un alma (a soul)
Adjectives stay feminine: el agua fría, mucha agua

examples.es: El agua está fría pero las aguas del mar están calientes.
examples.en: The water is cold but the sea waters are warm.
examples.note: singular el agua, plural las aguas — always feminine

mistakes.note: a + el always contracts to al.
Drop the article with an unmodified profession after ser.
el agua for sound, but the noun is feminine (fría).

mistakes.wrong: la problema / un problema... la mapa
mistakes.note: Match the article to the real gender, not just the ending.

title: Plurals — making nouns and articles plural
summary: Add -s after a vowel and -es after a consonant. Watch the spelling changes (-z to -ces) and remember to make the article and adjective plural too.
intro: Forming plurals in Spanish is regular and predictable — far simpler than the gender system. The main decision is whether to add -s or -es, and that depends on the last sound of the word.
The key thing English speakers forget: when the noun goes plural, everything around it goes plural too. The article (el → los) and any adjective (rojo → rojos) must follow.
formula: ends in vowel → + s  •  ends in consonant → + es

sections.heading: Rule 1 — words ending in a vowel add -s
bullets: libro → libros (books)
casa → casas (houses)
coche → coches (cars)
café → cafés (a stressed final vowel still just adds -s)

examples.es: Tengo dos hermanos y tres primos.
examples.en: I have two brothers and three cousins.

sections.heading: Rule 2 — words ending in a consonant add -es
bullets: ciudad → ciudades (cities)
profesor → profesores (teachers)
papel → papeles (papers)
rey → reyes (kings) — final -y counts as a consonant here

examples.es: Hay muchas ciudades grandes en España.
examples.en: There are many big cities in Spain.

sections.heading: Spelling changes you must watch
body: Two patterns trip up beginners: words ending in -z, and accents on the final syllable.
bullets: Final -z becomes -ces: lápiz → lápices, luz → luces, vez → veces
An accent on the last syllable usually disappears in the plural: nación → naciones, inglés → ingleses
Words ending in -s with no stress on the last syllable don't change: el lunes → los lunes, la crisis → las crisis

examples.es: Necesito dos lápices y tres luces nuevas.
examples.en: I need two pencils and three new lights.

examples.note: el lunes / los lunes — same form

sections.heading: Don't forget the article and adjective
body: A plural noun pulls its whole group into the plural. This is the most common slip for English speakers.
bullets: el coche rojo → los coches rojos
la casa blanca → las casas blancas
una idea buena → unas ideas buenas

examples.note: la→las, casa→casas, blanca→blancas — all plural and feminine

mistakes.note: Final -z changes to -ces.
ciudad is feminine, so the article is las.
The adjective must agree in number too.

title: Subject Pronouns — yo, tú, él…
summary: The words for I, you, he, she, we and they — usually dropped, because the verb ending already shows who is acting. Knowing them is still essential for emphasis and for choosing the right verb form.
intro: Subject pronouns tell you who performs the action. The biggest difference from English is that Spanish normally leaves them out: the verb ending already carries that information. 'Hablo español' on its own means 'I speak Spanish' — no yo needed.

So why learn them at all? Three reasons: (1) they tell you which verb ending to use, (2) you bring them back for emphasis or contrast, and (3) the formal/informal distinction (tú vs usted) is a real social choice you must make.
Spanish also distinguishes 'you all' as one word, and even marks the gender of a group with nosotros/nosotras and ellos/ellas.
formula: yo · tú · él/ella/usted · nosotros(as) · vosotros(as) · ellos/ellas/ustedes

sections.heading: The complete set
bullets: yo = I
tú = you (one person, informal)
él = he · ella = she · usted = you (one person, formal)
nosotros / nosotras = we (mixed or all-female group)
vosotros / vosotras = you all (informal, mainly Spain)
ellos / ellas = they · ustedes = you all (formal in Spain, all 'you all' in Latin America)

examples.note: ellos → a group with at least one male

sections.heading: Why pronouns are usually dropped
body: Because each verb ending is unique to a subject, repeating the pronoun sounds heavy or unnatural. Use the bare verb by default.
bullets: hablo = I speak · hablas = you speak · habla = he/she speaks
Add the pronoun only for emphasis, contrast or clarity

examples.en: I speak English and study Spanish.
examples.note: no yo — the -o endings already mean 'I'
pronouns added for contrast between two people

sections.heading: tú vs usted — the politeness choice
body: This is a social decision, not just grammar. Get it wrong and you can sound too cold or too casual.
bullets: tú → friends, family, classmates, children, people your own age
usted → strangers, elders, customers, a boss, anyone you want to show respect to
Important: usted uses the SAME verb form as él/ella (usted tiene, not usted tienes)
When unsure with an adult you don't know, start with usted

examples.note: usted → formal, uses the él/ella form necesita

sections.heading: vosotros vs ustedes — 'you all'
body: Spanish has a dedicated plural 'you'. Which one you use depends on the region.
bullets: In Spain: vosotros/vosotras = informal you all; ustedes = formal you all
In Latin America: ustedes covers ALL 'you all', formal or informal (vosotros isn't used)
ustedes takes the same verb form as ellos/ellas

examples.en: Are you all coming to the party? (Spain, informal)

mistakes.wrong: Yo soy yo voy a casa.
mistakes.note: Don't pile up or overuse yo — drop it unless you need emphasis.
tú has its own verb form (eres), not the él form (es).
usted uses the él/ella form (tiene), not the tú form.

title: Present Tense — regular -ar, -er, -ir verbs
summary: Spanish verbs belong to three families (-ar, -er, -ir). Drop the ending to get the stem, then add a new ending for each subject. One present tense covers both 'I speak' and 'I am speaking'.
intro: Every Spanish verb in the dictionary ends in -ar, -er or -ir. This ending decides which set of endings you'll add. To conjugate, you chop off the last two letters to get the 'stem', then attach the ending that matches your subject.

Good news for English speakers: the Spanish present tense does the work of several English structures. 'Hablo' can mean 'I speak', 'I am speaking', and 'I do speak'. You don't need a separate continuous form for most situations.
The -er and -ir endings are almost identical — they only differ in the nosotros and vosotros forms — so once you learn -ar and -er, -ir is nearly free.
formula: stem + ending (the ending shows who acts and replaces the need for a pronoun)

sections.heading: -ar verbs: hablar (to speak)
bullets: yo hablo · tú hablas · él/ella/usted habla
nosotros hablamos · vosotros habláis · ellos/ustedes hablan

sections.heading: -er verbs: comer (to eat)
bullets: yo como · tú comes · él/ella/usted come
nosotros comemos · vosotros coméis · ellos/ustedes comen

sections.heading: -ir verbs: vivir (to live)
body: Notice these match -er endings everywhere except nosotros (-imos) and vosotros (-ís).
bullets: yo vivo · tú vives · él/ella/usted vive
nosotros vivimos · vosotros vivís · ellos/ustedes viven

sections.heading: What the present tense expresses
body: This single tense stretches across several English meanings.
bullets: Habitual actions: Estudio español los lunes. (I study Spanish on Mondays.)
Current/ongoing actions: ¿Qué haces? — Leo un libro. (What are you doing? — I'm reading a book.)
General truths: El sol sale por el este. (The sun rises in the east.)
Near-future plans (with a time marker): Mañana viajo a Madrid. (Tomorrow I travel to Madrid.)

examples.es: Todos los días corro en el parque.
examples.en: Every day I run in the park.

examples.es: Este fin de semana visito a mi abuela.
examples.en: This weekend I'm visiting my grandmother.
examples.note: present used for a near-future plan

sections.heading: A quick note on common irregular verbs
body: A few very frequent verbs don't follow the pattern. You'll meet them constantly, so it helps to recognise them now — several have their own lessons.
bullets: ser (to be): soy, eres, es, somos, sois, son
estar (to be): estoy, estás, está, estamos, estáis, están
ir (to go): voy, vas, va, vamos, vais, van
tener (to have): tengo, tienes, tiene, tenemos, tenéis, tienen

examples.es: Voy al trabajo y estoy cansado.
examples.en: I go to work and I'm tired.

mistakes.note: Don't add ser — hablo already means 'I speak'.
Match the ending to the subject: nosotros → comemos.

mistakes.wrong: Estoy hablando español ahora... (when you just mean 'I speak')
mistakes.note: The simple present already covers ongoing actions; reserve estar + -ando for emphasising 'right now'.

title: Ser vs Estar — two verbs for ‘to be’
summary: Spanish splits English 'to be' into two verbs. Ser describes essence and identity; estar describes states, location and feelings. The same adjective can change meaning depending on which one you pick.
intro: English gets by with a single verb 'to be'. Spanish uses two — ser and estar — and choosing the wrong one can change your meaning or simply sound foreign. This is one of the genuine challenges of early Spanish, so it deserves real attention.
The cleanest mental model: ser tells you WHAT something fundamentally is (its identity, its nature). Estar tells you HOW or WHERE something is right now (its condition, its position).

Both verbs are irregular, so learn their forms first, then drill the difference with lots of examples. A useful memory aid for ser is DOCTOR (Description, Occupation, Characteristic, Time, Origin, Relationship); for estar, PLACE (Position, Location, Action in progress, Condition, Emotion).
formula: ser → identity / origin / time / permanent traits  •  estar → location / condition / feelings / ongoing actions

sections.heading: The forms (both irregular)
bullets: ser: soy · eres · es · somos · sois · son
estar: estoy · estás · está · estamos · estáis · están

examples.es: Soy de España, pero estoy en México ahora.
examples.en: I am from Spain, but I'm in Mexico now.

sections.heading: Use ser for identity and essence
bullets: Who/what something is: Soy profesor. (I am a teacher.)
Origin and nationality: Ella es de Colombia. Es colombiana.
Time, dates, days: Son las tres. Hoy es lunes.
Material and possession: La mesa es de madera. El libro es de Ana.
Permanent/defining traits: El hielo es frío. Mi hermano es alto.

examples.es: Mi hermana es alta y muy inteligente.
examples.en: My sister is tall and very intelligent.

examples.es: Son las ocho de la mañana.

sections.heading: Use estar for states and location
bullets: Physical location: Madrid está en España. El gato está en el jardín.
Temporary states and conditions: La sopa está caliente. La puerta está abierta.
Feelings and moods: Estoy cansado. Estamos felices.
Ongoing actions (estar + gerund): Estoy comiendo. Están trabajando.

examples.es: Los niños están jugando en el parque.
examples.en: The children are playing in the park.

sections.heading: Same adjective, different meaning
body: This is where the two verbs become a tool: switching ser/estar changes the message.
bullets: ser aburrido = to be boring · estar aburrido = to be bored
ser listo = to be clever · estar listo = to be ready
ser rico = to be rich · estar rico = to taste delicious
ser guapo = to be good-looking (a trait) · estar guapo = to look great (today)

examples.es: La película es aburrida, por eso estoy aburrido.
examples.en: The film is boring, that's why I'm bored.

sections.heading: A useful rule of thumb
body: When you're unsure, ask: is this a defining fact about what it IS, or a state it's IN right now?
bullets: Defining fact → ser (Es médico. La nieve es blanca.)
Current/changeable state → estar (Está enfermo. El cielo está gris hoy.)
Location of a thing or person → always estar (even something permanent like a city)

examples.es: El edificio es muy antiguo y está en el centro.
examples.en: The building is very old and is in the centre.
examples.note: es antiguo (trait) but está en el centro (location)

mistakes.note: Tiredness is a state → estar.
Location always uses estar, even for permanent places.
Events 'take place' → ser, even though it feels like location/time.
Profession/identity → ser.

title: Tener — to have (and key expressions)
summary: Tener means 'to have'. It is irregular, and many feelings that English builds with 'to be' (hungry, cold, afraid, … years old) are built with tener in Spanish.
intro: Tener (to have) is one of the most useful verbs you'll learn, and you'll use it from your very first conversations. It is irregular, so its forms need memorising.

Beyond simple possession, tener appears in a long list of fixed expressions where English uses the verb 'to be'. You don't say 'I am hungry' — you say 'I have hunger' (tengo hambre). These idioms are extremely common, so learning them early pays off fast.
One more high-value pattern: tener que + infinitive, which is the everyday way to say 'I have to / I must do something'.
formula: tengo · tienes · tiene · tenemos · tenéis · tienen

sections.heading: The forms
body: The yo form (tengo) and the stem change (e→ie) are the irregular parts.
bullets: yo tengo · tú tienes · él/ella/usted tiene
nosotros tenemos · vosotros tenéis · ellos/ustedes tienen

sections.heading: Feelings and states with tener
body: These use a noun, not an adjective: literally 'to have hunger', 'to have cold'. Intensify them with mucho/mucha, not muy.
bullets: tener hambre / sed = to be hungry / thirsty
tener frío / calor = to be cold / hot
tener sueño = to be sleepy · tener prisa = to be in a hurry
tener miedo (de) = to be afraid (of) · tener razón = to be right
tener suerte = to be lucky · tener cuidado = to be careful

examples.note: intensify with mucha (a noun), not muy

examples.es: Ten cuidado, el suelo está mojado.
examples.en: Be careful, the floor is wet.

sections.heading: Talking about age
body: Age uses tener, never ser. You literally 'have' a number of years.
bullets: tener … años = to be … years old
Question: ¿Cuántos años tienes? (How old are you?)

sections.heading: tener que + infinitive = to have to / must
body: This is the standard, everyday way to express obligation. Just add tener que before the base form of another verb.
bullets: Tengo que estudiar. (I have to study.)
Tienes que llamar a tu madre. (You have to call your mother.)
Tenemos que salir ahora. (We have to leave now.)

examples.en: Do you have to leave already?

mistakes.note: Hunger uses tener: literally 'I have hunger'.
Age uses tener, not ser.
hambre is a noun, so intensify with mucha, not muy.
tener que is followed directly by the infinitive — no extra a.

title: Gustar — saying what you like
summary: Gustar doesn't translate as 'to like' — it means 'to be pleasing to'. So the grammar is reversed: the thing you like is the subject, and you become an indirect object (me, te, le…).
intro: Gustar is famous for confusing learners, because it works backwards from English. It literally means 'to be pleasing'. So 'I like coffee' is expressed as 'Coffee is pleasing to me' → Me gusta el café.

The consequence: the thing being liked is the grammatical subject, so it controls the verb (singular gusta vs plural gustan). The person doing the liking is expressed with an indirect object pronoun (me, te, le, nos, os, les).
Once this 'flip' clicks, a whole family of verbs opens up — encantar (to love), interesar (to interest), molestar (to bother), doler (to hurt) all work exactly the same way.
formula: (a + person) + me/te/le/nos/os/les + gusta + singular thing  •  gustan + plural things

sections.heading: gusta vs gustan — match the thing, not the person
body: The verb agrees with what is liked, because that is the real subject.
bullets: One thing → gusta: Me gusta el chocolate.
Several things → gustan: Me gustan los perros.
An action (one or more verbs) → always gusta: Me gusta bailar. Me gusta leer y escribir.

examples.es: Me gustan los libros de aventuras.

examples.note: an action → gusta (even though you might think plural)

sections.heading: The indirect object pronouns (who likes it)
bullets: me = to me · te = to you (informal)
le = to him / her / you (formal)
nos = to us · os = to you all (Spain)
les = to them / you all

sections.heading: Adding 'a + person' for emphasis or clarity
body: You can put 'a mí', 'a ti', 'a Juan' etc. at the start. It's required to clarify the vague le/les (which could mean him, her, you, or them), and optional elsewhere for emphasis or contrast.
bullets: A mí me gusta el té. (I like tea — emphatic 'I')
A Juan le gusta el fútbol. (Juan likes football — clarifies le)
A mí me gusta el rojo, pero a ti te gusta el azul. (I like red, but you like blue — contrast)

examples.note: a María clarifies who 'le' refers to

examples.es: A mí me gustan los gatos, pero a él le gustan los perros.
examples.en: I like cats, but he likes dogs.

sections.heading: Stronger and related verbs
body: Same backwards structure, different shades of meaning. Note: with encantar you don't add 'muy' — the verb is already strong.
bullets: encantar = to love / really like: Me encanta el chocolate.
interesar = to interest: Me interesa la historia.
molestar = to bother: Me molesta el ruido.
doler = to hurt: Me duele la cabeza. (My head hurts.)

examples.es: A mi madre le duelen los pies.

mistakes.note: You don't 'do' the liking; the coffee is pleasing to you.
A plural thing needs gustan.
An action takes the singular gusta.
encantar is already strong — don't add mucho/muy.

title: Object Pronouns — lo, la, le, me, te…
summary: Pronouns that replace the person or thing receiving the action. Spanish splits them into direct (lo, la, los, las) and indirect (le, les), and they usually go BEFORE the verb.
intro: Once you can build sentences, you'll want to stop repeating nouns: 'I see the book → I see it'. Those replacement words are object pronouns. Spanish has two sets, and their position is different from English.
Direct objects answer 'what?' or 'whom?' (the thing directly acted on). Indirect objects answer 'to/for whom?' (the recipient). English often hides this difference; Spanish marks it clearly.
The golden rule of position: object pronouns normally come immediately BEFORE a conjugated verb — not after it as in English.
formula: pronoun + conjugated verb  (e.g. Lo veo = I see it)

sections.heading: Direct object pronouns (the thing acted on)
bullets: me = me · te = you · lo = him/it (masc.) · la = her/it (fem.)
nos = us · os = you all · los = them (masc.) · las = them (fem.)
lo/la/los/las must match the gender and number of the noun replaced

examples.en: The book? I have it here.
The keys? I don't see them.

sections.heading: Indirect object pronouns (the recipient)
body: These answer 'to whom / for whom'. Note le and les don't change for gender.
bullets: me = to me · te = to you · le = to him/her/you (formal)
nos = to us · os = to you all · les = to them/you all
Spanish often uses BOTH the pronoun and the named person: Le doy el libro a Juan.

examples.es: Le doy el regalo a mi madre.
examples.en: I give the gift to my mother.
examples.note: le AND 'a mi madre' both appear — normal in Spanish

sections.heading: Where the pronoun goes
body: Position is the part English speakers most often get wrong.
bullets: Before a conjugated verb: Lo quiero. (I want it.)
With an infinitive or gerund, you may attach it to the end: Quiero verlo. / Lo quiero ver. — both correct.
Attached to affirmative commands: ¡Cómelo! (Eat it!) — but before negative ones: ¡No lo comas!

examples.en: I'm going to call you tomorrow.
examples.note: attached to the infinitive: llamar + te

sections.heading: Using both pronouns together (le → se)
body: When an indirect and a direct pronoun meet, the indirect comes first. And if both would start with l- (le lo), the indirect changes to se.
bullets: Order: indirect + direct → Me lo das. (You give it to me.)
le/les + lo/la/los/las → se lo/la…: Se lo doy. (I give it to him/her.)

examples.en: The book? I'll lend it to you.

mistakes.note: The pronoun goes before the conjugated verb.
le + lo always becomes se lo.
The pronoun must match gender: llaves (fem.) → las.

title: Adjective Agreement
summary: Adjectives change their ending to match the noun's gender and number, and most of them come AFTER the noun. A few short ones change form when they go before it.
intro: In Spanish, an adjective is not a fixed word — it bends to match the noun it describes, in both gender and number. So 'red' is rojo, roja, rojos, or rojas depending on what it describes.

Position is the other surprise for English speakers: descriptive adjectives normally come AFTER the noun. 'A red car' is un coche rojo (literally 'a car red'), and 'an interesting book' is un libro interesante.
Get agreement right and your Spanish instantly sounds more natural; get it wrong and even simple sentences feel off.

sections.heading: Adjectives ending in -o have four forms
bullets: masculine singular: rojo → el coche rojo
feminine singular: roja → la casa roja
masculine plural: rojos → los coches rojos
feminine plural: rojas → las casas rojas

examples.es: El coche rojo y la casa roja son de mi tío.
examples.en: The red car and the red house belong to my uncle.
examples.note: rojo/roja agree with coche (m.) and casa (f.)

sections.heading: Adjectives ending in -e or a consonant
body: These do NOT change for gender — only for number. That makes them easier.
bullets: -e: grande → un libro grande, una casa grande (no gender change)
Plural of -e: add -s → libros grandes, casas grandes
Consonant ending: azul → un coche azul, una flor azul; plural azules
Exception — nationality adjectives ending in a consonant DO add -a for feminine: español → española, francés → francesa

examples.es: Una idea interesante y unos planes interesantes.
examples.en: An interesting idea and some interesting plans.

examples.es: Ella es española y él es francés.
examples.en: She is Spanish and he is French.

sections.heading: Position: usually after the noun
body: Most adjectives follow the noun. A small group of common ones can go before, sometimes with a shift in meaning.
bullets: Normal descriptive position: una película interesante, un día largo
Numbers and quantity words go before: tres libros, muchas personas, otra vez
Some change meaning by position: un viejo amigo (a long-standing friend) vs un amigo viejo (an elderly friend); un gran hombre (a great man) vs un hombre grande (a big man)

examples.note: grande → gran before the noun, meaning 'great'

examples.en: I live in a big house.
examples.note: after the noun, grande means physical size

sections.heading: Adjectives that shorten before a masculine noun
body: A few high-frequency adjectives drop their final -o right before a masculine singular noun.
bullets: bueno → buen: un buen amigo (a good friend)
malo → mal: un mal día (a bad day)
primero → primer, tercero → tercer: el primer día, el tercer piso
grande → gran (before ANY singular noun, m. or f.): una gran idea

examples.note: bueno → buen before a masculine singular noun

examples.es: Es el primer libro de la serie.
examples.en: It's the first book in the series.

mistakes.note: casa is feminine, so the adjective must be roja.
Plural noun needs a plural adjective.
Descriptive adjectives normally follow the noun.
bueno shortens to buen before a masculine singular noun.
